subtopic_detail: Sickle cell vaso-occlusive crisis - initial management
stem: A 7-year-old boy with known HbSS sickle cell disease presents to a metropolitan ED with 6 hours of severe pain in both thighs and the lower back. He has had three similar admissions in the past 18 months. He is afebrile, with no cough, no chest pain, no neurological symptoms and no abdominal pain. He takes daily penicillin V prophylaxis, folic acid and hydroxyurea, and his vaccinations including conjugate pneumococcal, meningococcal B and ACWY, Hib and annual influenza are up to date. He last had a transfusion 9 months ago. On examination he is alert and engaged, tearful with pain, with no scleral icterus, a soft non-tender abdomen, no chest signs and no neurological deficit. There is no swelling of joints, no warmth and no focal tenderness over long bones beyond diffuse muscular tenderness. Initial observations: T 36.9 degC, HR 118 bpm, RR 22, SpO2 98% on room air, BP 108/64 mmHg. Bedside SpO2 trend over the last 30 minutes is stable. He has not taken any analgesia at home and is rating his pain 9 out of 10.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.9 degC, HR 118, RR 22, SpO2 98% RA, BP 108/64.
data_table.Examination: Distressed by pain, no chest signs, no focal bony tenderness, no scleral icterus, no neurology.
data_table.Medications: Penicillin V prophylaxis, folic acid, hydroxyurea.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Oral ibuprofen alone, then reassess pain at 60 minutes
options.rationale: Oral NSAID monotherapy is inadequate for severe (9/10) sickle pain; guidance is to give a strong opioid within 30 minutes alongside multimodal analgesia (RCH CPG: Sickle cell disease).

options.text: IV saline bolus 20 mL/kg, observe without giving analgesia
options.rationale: Bolus fluids are not first-line in uncomplicated vaso-occlusive crisis and risk pulmonary oedema and acute chest syndrome; maintenance fluids do not replace timely opioid analgesia (RCH CPG: Sickle cell disease; BSH 2018 guideline).
source_refs: BSH guideline: Acute management of sickle cell pain

options.text: IV morphine plus paracetamol and ibuprofen, maintenance fluids
options.rationale: Severe vaso-occlusive crisis requires a strong opioid (IV or intranasal) within 30 minutes, combined with paracetamol and an NSAID if no contraindication, plus maintenance (not bolus) fluids. Oxygen is given only if SpO2 falls below 95% (RCH CPG: Sickle cell disease).

options.text: Empirical IV ceftriaxone, blood cultures and ward admission
options.rationale: He is afebrile with no infective focus; empirical antibiotics are reserved for sickle cell patients with temperature 38 degC or higher (RCH CPG: Sickle cell disease).

options.text: Urgent exchange transfusion via the on-call haematologist
options.rationale: Exchange transfusion is indicated for acute chest syndrome, stroke, multi-organ failure or refractory priapism - not for uncomplicated vaso-occlusive pain (RCH CPG: Sickle cell disease; BSH guideline).
source_refs: BSH guideline: Acute management of sickle cell pain

explanation.summary: Uncomplicated paediatric vaso-occlusive crisis is managed with timely strong opioid analgesia (target within 30 minutes), multimodal paracetamol and NSAID, maintenance IV fluids, and supplemental oxygen only if SpO2 falls below 95% (RCH CPG: Sickle cell disease).
key_points: Strong opioid within 30 minutes for severe sickle pain
Maintenance fluids, not boluses, in uncomplicated VOC
Oxygen only if SpO2 < 95% to avoid masking acute chest syndrome
Reassess every 30 minutes for the first 2 hours
Screen for triggers: dehydration, infection, hypoxia, cold
explanation.pearls: Bolus fluids and high-flow oxygen in a normoxic child with sickle pain can both precipitate acute chest syndrome - give what is needed, not more.

sources.label: BSH guideline: Acute management of sickle cell pain

subtopic_detail: Acute chest syndrome recognition in sickle cell disease
stem: A 9-year-old girl with HbSS sickle cell disease was admitted 36 hours ago with a vaso-occlusive crisis affecting her back and hips, and has been on IV morphine via PCA, regular paracetamol and ibuprofen, and maintenance fluids at 100% maintenance. Pain has improved from 9 to 5 out of 10. Overnight she developed a new dry cough and right-sided pleuritic chest pain on inspiration. This morning her temperature is 38.4 degC, HR 132 bpm, RR 36, SpO2 91% on room air rising to 95% on 2 L nasal prongs, BP 104/62 mmHg. On auscultation she has reduced air entry and crackles at the right base. She has had no vomiting, no recent travel, and no sick contacts. She is fully vaccinated and on penicillin V prophylaxis and hydroxyurea. Her last transfusion was 8 months ago. A chest X-ray performed in the last hour shows a new right lower zone consolidation that was not present on admission. Repeat FBC shows Hb 71 g/L (baseline 88), WCC 19.4 x10^9/L, platelets 312, reticulocytes 320 x10^9/L, CRP 96 mg/L.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.4 degC, HR 132, RR 36, SpO2 91% RA / 95% on 2 L NP, BP 104/62.
data_table.Examination: Reduced air entry and crackles right base, distressed by pain and dyspnoea.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 71 g/L (baseline 88), WCC 19.4, platelets 312, CRP 96. CXR: new right lower zone consolidation.
data_table.Medications: IV morphine PCA, paracetamol, ibuprofen, penicillin V, hydroxyurea, maintenance saline.

lead_in: Which is the single best next step?

options.text: IV ceftriaxone plus azithromycin, oxygen, incentive spirometry, urgent haematology call regarding transfusion
options.rationale: New infiltrate plus fever, hypoxia and respiratory signs in sickle cell disease defines acute chest syndrome. Management is empirical antibiotics (third-generation cephalosporin plus macrolide), oxygen to keep SpO2 above 94%, incentive spirometry, judicious fluids, and urgent transfusion (simple or exchange) directed by haematology (RCH CPG: Sickle cell disease; BSH guideline).
source_refs: BSH guideline: Management of acute chest syndrome

options.text: Increase morphine PCA background rate and continue current ward management
options.rationale: Opioid titration alone misses the diagnosis of acute chest syndrome, and excess sedation worsens hypoventilation and atelectasis, perpetuating the cycle (RCH CPG: Sickle cell disease).

options.text: Give a 20 mL/kg normal saline bolus and repeat the chest X-ray in 6 hours
options.rationale: Fluid boluses are contraindicated in evolving acute chest syndrome because they worsen pulmonary oedema; fluids should be cut to two-thirds maintenance and treatment escalated now, not deferred (RCH CPG: Sickle cell disease).

options.text: Stop ibuprofen, give nebulised salbutamol and observe on the ward
options.rationale: There is no wheeze or known asthma diagnosis to support salbutamol as primary therapy, and ward-level observation without antibiotics or transfusion planning is inappropriate for evolving acute chest syndrome (BSH guideline).
source_refs: BSH guideline: Management of acute chest syndrome

options.text: Arrange urgent CT pulmonary angiogram before any further therapy
options.rationale: CTPA is reserved for cases where pulmonary embolism is genuinely suspected; the new infiltrate, fever and hypoxia after a sickle admission are diagnostic of acute chest syndrome and treatment should not be delayed for imaging (RCH CPG: Sickle cell disease).

explanation.summary: Acute chest syndrome is defined by a new radiographic infiltrate plus fever or respiratory signs in a child with sickle cell disease and is the leading cause of sickle mortality. Management is parallel: antibiotics with atypical cover, oxygen, incentive spirometry, conservative fluids, and early haematology-led transfusion (RCH CPG: Sickle cell disease).
key_points: New infiltrate + fever / hypoxia / respiratory signs = ACS
Antibiotics: cefotaxime/ceftriaxone + macrolide
Oxygen to SpO2 > 94%, incentive spirometry hourly while awake
Fluids two-thirds maintenance
Simple transfusion if Hb < 90; exchange if severe / no response
explanation.pearls: Post-admission ACS usually develops 24-72 hours into a VOC admission as splinting and opioids cause atelectasis - prevent it with spirometry.

sources.label: BSH guideline: Management of acute chest syndrome

subtopic_detail: Discriminating sickle crisis types - sequestration vs aplastic
stem: A 3-year-old girl with known HbSS sickle cell disease, on daily penicillin V prophylaxis and folic acid, is brought to a regional ED by her mother. Over the last 24 hours she has become progressively lethargic, with poor oral intake but no vomiting or diarrhoea. Her older brother had a febrile illness with a malar rash one week ago. She has not had any cough, dyspnoea, chest or limb pain, no preceding bony pain and no haematuria. She is afebrile today, but two days ago had a low-grade temperature. Vaccinations are up to date and her last transfusion was 4 months ago. On examination she is pale and tired, alert but quiet, with no scleral icterus, no rash, clear chest and no neurology. Her abdomen is soft with a non-tender spleen palpable 2 cm below the costal margin, unchanged from her baseline. Observations: T 36.7 degC, HR 142, RR 28, SpO2 99% RA, BP 92/56, capillary refill 2 s. Bedside bloods: Hb 48 g/L (baseline 85), WCC 6.8 x10^9/L, platelets 256, reticulocytes 12 x10^9/L (baseline approximately 300), bilirubin 24 micromol/L, LDH mildly elevated. Mother brought her in because of pallor noticed at bath time.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7 degC, HR 142, RR 28, SpO2 99% RA, BP 92/56, cap refill 2 s.
data_table.Examination: Pale, alert, spleen 2 cm below costal margin (baseline), no jaundice, no haemodynamic compromise.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 48 g/L (baseline 85), reticulocytes 12 x10^9/L (baseline ~300), WCC 6.8, platelets 256, bilirubin 24 micromol/L.

lead_in: Which diagnosis best explains this presentation?

options.text: Acute splenic sequestration crisis
options.rationale: Splenic sequestration is characterised by a sudden enlargement of the spleen by 2 cm or more from baseline with a brisk reticulocyte response and signs of hypovolaemic shock; this child has an unchanged spleen and a suppressed reticulocyte count (RCH CPG: Sickle cell disease).

options.text: Hyperhaemolytic crisis from delayed transfusion reaction
options.rationale: Hyperhaemolysis presents with jaundice, dark urine, raised LDH and bilirubin and a brisk reticulocytosis, typically within 1-3 weeks of transfusion; her last transfusion was 4 months ago and the reticulocyte count is suppressed (BSH guideline).
source_refs: BSH guideline: Transfusion in sickle cell disease

options.text: Iron deficiency anaemia from poor dietary intake
options.rationale: Iron deficiency develops over weeks to months and would not drop the haemoglobin by nearly half in 24 hours; reticulocytes in chronic iron deficiency are typically normal rather than profoundly suppressed (RCH CPG: Iron deficiency).

options.text: Aplastic crisis from parvovirus B19 infection
options.rationale: A rapid fall in haemoglobin with an inappropriately low reticulocyte count (here 12 against a baseline of approximately 300 x10^9/L) in a child with chronic haemolysis is diagnostic of an aplastic crisis. Parvovirus B19, the cause of fifth disease (the brother's malar rash), suppresses erythroid precursors for 7-10 days and is the typical precipitant (RCH CPG: Sickle cell disease; eviQ).

options.text: Vaso-occlusive bone marrow infarction
options.rationale: Marrow infarction causes severe pain rather than asymptomatic anaemia, and is usually associated with leucocytosis and an appropriate reticulocyte response (RCH CPG: Sickle cell disease).

explanation.summary: Aplastic crisis in sickle cell disease is a sudden drop in haemoglobin with a suppressed reticulocyte count (typically < 50 x10^9/L), most often triggered by parvovirus B19 which transiently halts erythropoiesis. It is distinguished from sequestration (sudden splenomegaly, hypovolaemia, reticulocytosis) by the reticulocyte count and spleen size (RCH CPG: Sickle cell disease).
key_points: Aplastic: suppressed retics + unchanged spleen + parvovirus contact
Sequestration: spleen enlarged 2+ cm + shock + reticulocytosis
Hyperhaemolysis: post-transfusion + jaundice + brisk retics
Manage aplastic crisis with transfusion and parvovirus isolation
Isolate from pregnant staff/family until resolution
explanation.pearls: A fifth-disease rash in a sibling is the classic clue - the index child may never have been febrile.

sources.label: BSH guideline: Transfusion in sickle cell disease

subtopic_detail: Acute childhood ITP - watchful waiting threshold
stem: A 4-year-old previously well girl is brought to the GP after her parents noticed widespread petechiae on her shins and a few bruises on her trunk that appeared over 48 hours. She had a coryzal illness 10 days ago that has now resolved. She is otherwise well, eating, drinking, and playing normally. There has been no fever in the past week, no bone pain, no night sweats and no weight loss. She has no significant past medical history, no recent travel and her vaccinations are up to date. Family history is negative for bleeding disorders. On examination she is well, alert and playful. Temperature is 36.8 degC, HR 102, RR 22, BP 96/58, capillary refill 2 s. She has scattered petechiae on both lower legs and four bruises 1-2 cm across on the trunk and arms in non-suspicious distributions. There is no mucosal bleeding, no epistaxis and no lymphadenopathy. There is no hepatosplenomegaly and no joint swelling. Bedside FBC: Hb 124 g/L, WCC 7.2 x10^9/L with normal differential, neutrophils 3.4, lymphocytes 3.1, platelets 18 x10^9/L. Blood film shows large platelets with otherwise normal red and white cell morphology.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 degC, HR 102, RR 22, BP 96/58, cap refill 2 s.
data_table.Examination: Well, playful. Petechiae shins, scattered trunk bruises. No mucosal bleeding, no lymphadenopathy, no organomegaly.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 124 g/L, WCC 7.2 (normal diff), platelets 18 x10^9/L. Film: large platelets, otherwise normal.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Admit and infuse IV immunoglobulin 0.8 g/kg now
options.rationale: IVIG is reserved for ITP with significant mucosal bleeding or in preparation for a procedure; cutaneous-only bleeding does not meet the threshold for pharmacotherapy (RCH CPG: ITP).

options.text: Reassure, give safety advice and arrange paediatric follow-up
options.rationale: Typical acute childhood ITP with platelets above 10 x10^9/L, only cutaneous bleeding (petechiae and skin bruising) and no mucosal or internal bleeding is managed with watchful waiting, written advice on activity restriction and bleeding red flags, and outpatient paediatric follow-up with a repeat FBC at 7-10 days (RCH CPG: ITP).

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone 2 mg/kg/day for two weeks
options.rationale: Corticosteroids are an option for moderate bleeding but require bone marrow examination to exclude leukaemia before commencement and are not indicated for skin-only bleeding (RCH CPG: ITP; ASH 2019 ITP guideline).

options.text: Transfuse a single unit of pooled adult platelets
options.rationale: Platelet transfusion in ITP is rapidly consumed by autoantibodies and is reserved for life-threatening bleeding such as intracranial haemorrhage; it has no role in skin-only disease (RCH CPG: ITP).

options.text: Refer for urgent bone marrow aspirate and trephine
options.rationale: Bone marrow examination is only required if features atypical for ITP are present (hepatosplenomegaly, lymphadenopathy, cytopenias in other lines, abnormal film) or before commencing steroids; the picture here is classic for ITP (RCH CPG: ITP).

explanation.summary: Acute childhood ITP with cutaneous-only bleeding is managed expectantly because around 80% of children remit within 6 months. Pharmacotherapy (IVIG or steroids) is for moderate mucosal bleeding; platelet transfusion is reserved for life-threatening haemorrhage (RCH CPG: ITP).
key_points: Skin-only bleeding + platelets > 10 = watchful waiting
Wet purpura / mucosal bleeding = consider IVIG or steroids
Life-threatening bleed = IVIG + methylprednisolone + platelet transfusion + surgical input
Bone marrow before steroids (to exclude ALL) if treating
Safety advice: avoid contact sports, NSAIDs and IM injections
explanation.pearls: The platelet count predicts bleeding poorly - the wet vs dry purpura distinction is the better guide to therapy.

subtopic_detail: ITP with wet purpura - IVIG vs steroids decision
stem: A 6-year-old boy presents to ED with worsening epistaxis lasting 35 minutes despite firm nasal pinching, and new oral blood blisters noted by his mother this morning. Petechiae appeared over the legs and trunk 3 days ago. He had a mild diarrhoeal illness 2 weeks ago that resolved without antibiotics. He is otherwise well, with no fever, weight loss, night sweats or bone pain, and no joint swelling. Past history is unremarkable, vaccinations are up to date and he takes no medications. On examination he is alert, slightly pale, with active oozing from the left anterior nare, multiple buccal haemorrhagic bullae, scattered petechiae and bruising over the trunk and lower limbs, but no lymphadenopathy and no hepatosplenomegaly. Observations: T 36.9 degC, HR 118, RR 24, BP 102/64, SpO2 99% RA, capillary refill 2 s. FBC: Hb 102 g/L, WCC 8.0 x10^9/L with normal differential, platelets 4 x10^9/L. Coagulation: PT 12 s, APTT 32 s, fibrinogen 3.1 g/L. Blood film shows isolated thrombocytopenia with large platelets and otherwise normal cells. There has been no haematuria, no melaena and no neurological symptoms.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.9 degC, HR 118, RR 24, BP 102/64, SpO2 99% RA.
data_table.Examination: Active epistaxis 35 min, oral haemorrhagic bullae, petechiae and bruises. No lymphadenopathy or organomegaly.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 102 g/L, WCC 8.0 (normal diff), platelets 4 x10^9/L. Coags normal. Film: isolated thrombocytopenia, large platelets.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line treatment?

options.text: Continue nasal pressure and discharge with outpatient haematology follow-up
options.rationale: Wet purpura with prolonged active epistaxis crosses the threshold for inpatient pharmacotherapy and the child should not be discharged without treatment (RCH CPG: ITP).

options.text: IV immunoglobulin 0.8 g/kg with admission for observation
options.rationale: Significant mucosal (wet) bleeding warrants treatment. IVIG raises the platelet count fastest (within 24-48 hours) and avoids the need for marrow examination before steroids in typical ITP, and is the preferred first-line option in Australian paediatric guidance for moderate non-life-threatening bleeding (RCH CPG: ITP; ASH 2019 ITP guideline).

options.text: Pooled platelet transfusion (one adult dose) and admit
options.rationale: Transfused platelets are rapidly destroyed by anti-platelet antibodies and are reserved for life-threatening bleeding (intracranial, major GI). They are not first-line for epistaxis and wet purpura (RCH CPG: ITP).

options.text: Oral tranexamic acid alone with review in 48 hours
options.rationale: Tranexamic acid is a useful adjunct for mucosal bleeding but does not raise the platelet count and is inadequate as monotherapy at this severity (RCH CPG: ITP).

options.text: Splenectomy after initial haemodynamic stabilisation
options.rationale: Splenectomy is reserved for chronic refractory ITP after at least 12 months of failed medical therapy, not for acute presentations (ASH 2019 ITP guideline).

explanation.summary: Children with ITP who have wet purpura (mucosal bleeding such as epistaxis lasting > 15 minutes, oral bullae, haematuria or GI bleed) warrant pharmacotherapy. IVIG produces the most rapid platelet rise and is preferred over corticosteroids when bone marrow examination has not been done, because steroids can partially treat ALL and mask the diagnosis (RCH CPG: ITP).
key_points: Wet purpura = treat; dry purpura = observe
IVIG 0.8 g/kg raises platelets within 24-48 hours
Steroids work but require marrow first to exclude ALL
Tranexamic acid useful as mucosal adjunct, not monotherapy
Platelet transfusion only for life-threatening haemorrhage
explanation.pearls: If the bone marrow looks normal you can use either - but the marrow has to come first for steroids.

stem: An 8-year-old boy with newly-diagnosed acute ITP (platelets 6 x10^9/L on a community FBC two days ago) is brought to a tertiary ED at 22:00 after a witnessed fall from standing height onto a tiled floor. He struck the back of his head and had immediate vomiting, followed by 15 minutes of drowsiness. On arrival he is rousable to voice with a GCS of 12 (E3 V4 M5), HR 122, RR 22, BP 116/72, SpO2 99% room air, T 36.8 degC, pupils 3 mm equal and reactive, with no focal neurological deficit. He has scattered petechiae and bruising over the trunk and limbs, oozing from the gums, and a 3 cm haematoma over the right occiput. There is no other injury. He was not taking any antiplatelet or anticoagulant medication. CT head obtained 20 minutes ago shows a small right parietal acute subdural haematoma without midline shift. Repeat FBC in ED: Hb 108 g/L, WCC 7.4 with normal differential, platelets 5 x10^9/L. Coagulation: PT 12 s, APTT 30 s, fibrinogen 3.0 g/L. The neurosurgical registrar has been called.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 degC, HR 122, RR 22, BP 116/72, SpO2 99% RA, GCS 12.
data_table.Examination: Drowsy, occipital haematoma, gum ooze, petechiae and bruising. No focal neurology.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 108, WCC 7.4 (normal diff), platelets 5 x10^9/L. Coags normal. CT: small acute right parietal SDH, no mass effect.

lead_in: Which combination of immediate interventions is most appropriate?

options.text: IVIG 1 g/kg alone with observation in a paediatric high-dependency bed
options.rationale: Although IVIG is required, monotherapy is inadequate in life-threatening intracranial bleeding because it takes 24-48 hours to raise the platelet count; concurrent platelet transfusion and high-dose corticosteroid are needed (RCH CPG: ITP; ASH 2019 ITP guideline).

options.text: Platelet transfusion alone, admit for neurosurgical observation
options.rationale: Platelet transfusion alone is rapidly consumed by anti-platelet antibodies and recovery is incomplete; IVIG and methylprednisolone are required adjuncts (RCH CPG: ITP).

options.text: IV tranexamic acid bolus followed by infusion as principal therapy
options.rationale: Tranexamic acid may be used cautiously as an adjunct, but is not first-line for intracranial bleeding in ITP and does not raise the platelet count (ASH 2019 ITP guideline).

options.text: Platelet transfusion plus IV methylprednisolone plus IVIG, urgent neurosurgical review
options.rationale: Life-threatening bleeding (including intracranial haemorrhage) in ITP is treated with triple therapy: platelet transfusion (transient but immediate rise), IVIG 1 g/kg to block reticuloendothelial clearance, and high-dose IV methylprednisolone (30 mg/kg, max 1 g), with urgent neurosurgical review and PICU/HDU admission (RCH CPG: ITP; ASH 2019 ITP guideline).

options.text: Immediate emergency splenectomy for rapid platelet response
options.rationale: Emergency splenectomy is not a first-line response to ITP-related ICH and is reserved for chronic refractory disease; medical therapy and neurosurgical control take priority (ASH 2019 ITP guideline).

explanation.summary: Life-threatening bleeding in ITP, especially intracranial haemorrhage, is treated with combined platelet transfusion, IV methylprednisolone and IVIG, alongside specialty (neurosurgical / surgical) input. Each component addresses a different mechanism: rapid (transfusion), reticuloendothelial blockade (IVIG) and immunosuppression (steroid) (RCH CPG: ITP).
key_points: Triple therapy: platelets + IV methylprednisolone + IVIG
Add tranexamic acid as adjunct if no contraindication
PICU / HDU admission and urgent neurosurgical review
Repeat imaging and FBC at 6-12 hours
Reverse any antiplatelet or anticoagulant exposure
explanation.pearls: ICH is rare (< 1%) in childhood ITP but the case fatality is high - do not wait for the platelet count to rise on IVIG alone.

subtopic_detail: Definition and recognition of febrile neutropenia
stem: A 9-year-old boy on maintenance chemotherapy for B-cell ALL (currently 14 months into therapy) is brought to ED 2 hours after a single recorded oral temperature of 38.4 degC at home. He has had mild coryza but no localising features, no rigors, no rash and no diarrhoea. He took oral paracetamol 1 hour ago. He has a tunnelled central venous access device with no overlying erythema or tenderness. His most recent FBC, taken 3 days ago at clinic, showed Hb 102 g/L, WCC 1.2 x10^9/L, neutrophils 0.3 x10^9/L and platelets 138 x10^9/L. Observations on arrival are T 37.9 degC, HR 116, RR 22, SpO2 98% RA, BP 104/64, capillary refill 2 s. The triage nurse asks whether he meets criteria for febrile neutropenia.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.9 degC (38.4 at home), HR 116, RR 22, SpO2 98% RA, BP 104/64.
data_table.Examination: Well-appearing, central line site clean, mild coryza, no localising features.
data_table.Investigations: FBC 3 days ago: Hb 102, WCC 1.2, neutrophils 0.3 x10^9/L, platelets 138.
data_table.Medications: Maintenance chemotherapy for ALL (oral 6-MP, weekly oral methotrexate, intermittent vincristine and steroid pulses).

lead_in: Which statement best describes whether he meets the definition of febrile neutropenia?

options.text: No - he needs two temperatures 38.0 degC or higher one hour apart
options.rationale: A single recorded temperature 38.0 degC or higher is sufficient under current Australian guidance; the two-temperature rule is outdated (RCH CPG: Febrile neutropenia).

options.text: No - the threshold for neutropenia is below 1.0 x10^9/L
options.rationale: The cut-off for neutropenia in the definition is an absolute neutrophil count below 0.5 x10^9/L (some protocols include patients expected to drop below 0.5); his count of 0.3 is below this (RCH CPG: Febrile neutropenia).

options.text: No - paracetamol given at home invalidates the febrile reading
options.rationale: An antipyretic dose given at home does not exclude the diagnosis; the highest recorded temperature is used and empirical antibiotics are commenced regardless (RCH CPG: Febrile neutropenia).

options.text: No - he requires a positive blood culture to confirm the diagnosis
options.rationale: Febrile neutropenia is an empirical clinical syndrome defined by temperature and neutrophil count and does not require culture confirmation (RCH CPG: Febrile neutropenia).

options.text: Yes - a single temperature 38.0 degC or higher with ANC below 0.5
options.rationale: Australian paediatric oncology services define febrile neutropenia as a single recorded temperature 38.0 degC or higher in a child with an absolute neutrophil count below 0.5 x10^9/L (or expected to fall below this). This child qualifies and needs empirical IV antibiotics within 60 minutes (RCH CPG: Febrile neutropenia; eviQ).

explanation.summary: Febrile neutropenia in paediatric oncology is a single temperature 38.0 degC or higher with neutrophils below 0.5 x10^9/L (or expected to fall below). It is a medical emergency requiring empirical IV antibiotics within 60 minutes of arrival (RCH CPG: Febrile neutropenia).
key_points: Single T >=38.0 degC + ANC < 0.5 x10^9/L = febrile neutropenia
Door-to-antibiotic target: 60 minutes
Central line bundle: peripheral + line cultures
Antipyretic at home does not exclude the diagnosis
Some protocols also accept sustained 38.0 degC for 1 hour
explanation.pearls: If the child is on chemotherapy and warm to touch, start the FN pathway - do not wait for confirmation.

subtopic_detail: Empirical antibiotic choice in febrile neutropenia
stem: A 5-year-old girl with high-risk B-cell ALL on intensive consolidation chemotherapy presents to a tertiary paediatric ED at 03:00 with fever to 38.7 degC and 4 hours of rigors. Her last cycle of cytarabine finished 8 days ago. She has a tunnelled central venous catheter, which the parents have used for at-home nutrition. There is no cough, no diarrhoea, no rash and no abdominal pain. She has had no foreign travel, no known sick contacts and no documented MRSA or ESBL colonisation. Penicillin allergy was disproven by formal challenge last year. On arrival she is alert, with mild pallor, T 38.6 degC, HR 138, RR 28, SpO2 98% RA, BP 96/56, capillary refill 3 s. The central line exit site is clean with no erythema, tunnel tenderness or discharge. There is mild mucositis but no other focus. FBC: Hb 89 g/L, WCC 0.6 x10^9/L, neutrophils 0.1 x10^9/L, platelets 42 x10^9/L. Blood cultures from line and peripheral have been taken, urinalysis is unremarkable, and a chest X-ray is pending. The unit follows local guidelines aligned with the Therapeutic Guidelines.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.6 degC, HR 138, RR 28, SpO2 98% RA, BP 96/56, cap refill 3 s.
data_table.Examination: Mucositis only, central line clean, no focal signs.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 89, WCC 0.6, neutrophils 0.1 x10^9/L, platelets 42. Cultures sent.
data_table.Medications: Intensive consolidation chemotherapy (cytarabine completed 8 days ago).

lead_in: Which empirical antibiotic regimen is most appropriate?

options.text: IV ceftriaxone monotherapy at paediatric dose
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone lacks reliable activity against Pseudomonas aeruginosa, a key pathogen in febrile neutropenia, so it is not an acceptable empirical monotherapy (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

options.text: IV vancomycin plus IV gentamicin combined therapy
options.rationale: This combination misses anaerobes and does not provide reliable broad Gram-negative cover; vancomycin is added only if there is suspected line sepsis, mucositis-related streptococcal risk, or MRSA colonisation (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; RCH CPG).

options.text: IV piperacillin-tazobactam monotherapy at full dose
options.rationale: Australian Therapeutic Guidelines and RCH recommend empirical piperacillin-tazobactam monotherapy as first-line for febrile neutropenia in stable children with no line sepsis, abdominal source, MRSA colonisation or proven beta-lactam allergy, because it covers Pseudomonas, anaerobes and Gram-positives (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; RCH CPG: Febrile neutropenia).

options.text: Oral ciprofloxacin plus amoxicillin-clavulanate outpatient
options.rationale: Outpatient oral regimens are only offered to selected low-risk patients in research protocols and are not standard for high-risk ALL on intensive chemotherapy with neutrophils 0.1 (eviQ; Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: IV meropenem monotherapy as first-line empirical
options.rationale: Meropenem is reserved for sepsis, suspected ESBL, failure of first-line therapy or specific complicated infections; it is not first-line empirical therapy when piperacillin-tazobactam is suitable (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

explanation.summary: Empirical antibiotic therapy in paediatric febrile neutropenia in Australia is piperacillin-tazobactam monotherapy first-line, with vancomycin added for suspected line, skin/soft tissue or mucositis-related Gram-positive infection. Meropenem replaces piperacillin-tazobactam if the patient is unstable, has known ESBL colonisation or has had recent piperacillin-tazobactam exposure (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).
key_points: First-line: piperacillin-tazobactam monotherapy
Add vancomycin: line infection, severe mucositis, MRSA, shock
Escalate to meropenem: instability, ESBL, recent broad-spectrum
Door-to-antibiotic 60 minutes
Take line + peripheral cultures before first dose
explanation.pearls: If the child looks septic, escalate fluid and antibiotics in parallel - do not wait for the chest X-ray.

subtopic_detail: Septic workup priorities in febrile neutropenia
stem: A 12-year-old girl with AML in induction (day 11) presents to ED 90 minutes after a recorded oral temperature of 39.1 degC at home. She has a tunnelled central line in situ and at the exit site there is new erythema and tenderness. She has had one episode of vomiting and feels weak but reports no respiratory, urinary or abdominal symptoms. Triage observations: T 39.0 degC, HR 162, RR 34, SpO2 96% RA, BP 88/46, capillary refill 4 s. She appears pale and slightly mottled peripherally and is sleepy but rousable. Her most recent FBC, taken 24 hours ago, showed Hb 84, WCC 0.3, neutrophils 0.05 x10^9/L, platelets 28. She has no documented antibiotic allergies. She last had piperacillin-tazobactam 8 days ago for a previous febrile episode that was culture-negative. The PEWS triggers immediate review and the unit's paediatric sepsis pathway is activated. Repeat venous lactate at the bedside is 3.6 mmol/L and capillary glucose is 5.8 mmol/L. Her parents have a copy of her oncology plan listing penicillin tolerance and recent antibiotic exposure.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.0 degC, HR 162, RR 34, SpO2 96% RA, BP 88/46, cap refill 4 s.
data_table.Examination: Sleepy, mottled, central line exit site erythema and tenderness.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 84, WCC 0.3, neutrophils 0.05, platelets 28. Lactate 3.6 mmol/L, glucose 5.8 mmol/L.
data_table.Medications: AML induction chemotherapy (day 11). Previous pip-tazo course 8 days ago.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate priority?

options.text: Remove central venous line immediately and culture catheter tip before any antibiotics
options.rationale: Antibiotic delivery within 60 minutes takes priority over line removal; the decision to remove the line is made later in conjunction with oncology if cultures are positive or the patient deteriorates (eviQ).

options.text: Paired cultures plus IV meropenem and vancomycin with 20 mL/kg saline bolus, within 60 minutes
options.rationale: She has septic shock (hypotension, prolonged capillary refill, lactate 3.6) with suspected central line infection, recent piperacillin-tazobactam exposure and profound neutropenia. The bundle is paired peripheral and line cultures, broad escalation to meropenem with vancomycin to cover line-associated Gram-positives, and a 20 mL/kg crystalloid bolus, within 60 minutes (RCH CPG; ANZICS).

options.text: Urgent CT abdomen and pelvis to exclude typhlitis before commencing any antibiotics
options.rationale: Empirical antibiotics must not be delayed for imaging; CT for suspected typhlitis is considered after the patient is stabilised and only if abdominal signs are present (RCH CPG: Febrile neutropenia).

options.text: IV piperacillin-tazobactam alone, then reassess at 30 minutes before any escalation
options.rationale: Piperacillin-tazobactam was given 8 days ago for a similar episode and the child is in shock with line erythema; cover should be broadened to meropenem plus vancomycin from the outset (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

options.text: Give granulocyte colony-stimulating factor immediately to raise the neutrophil count first
options.rationale: G-CSF is not part of the acute septic resuscitation bundle and has no role in the first hour; resuscitation, cultures and antibiotics come first (eviQ).

explanation.summary: Septic shock in febrile neutropenia mandates a parallel bundle: paired peripheral and central line cultures, broad-spectrum antibiotics (escalated to meropenem plus vancomycin when there is line involvement, recent piperacillin-tazobactam exposure, or shock) and 20 mL/kg crystalloid bolus, all delivered within the first hour (RCH CPG: Febrile neutropenia; ANZICS).
key_points: Door-to-antibiotic 60 minutes regardless of culture results
Paired peripheral + line cultures before first dose
Add vancomycin for line involvement, shock, mucositis or MRSA
Escalate to meropenem if recent pip-tazo or instability
Line removal decided later, after stabilisation and culture data
explanation.pearls: If they look septic and have a line, peripheral access is more important than removing the line in the first 60 minutes.

stem: A 5-year-old previously well boy is brought to his GP with a 3-week history of intermittent fevers, fatigue, generalised limb pains worse at night that have caused him to wake crying, and pallor noted by his grandmother. He has been less active and is reluctant to walk to the letterbox. There is no preceding viral illness, no rash, no recent travel and no antibiotic use. Vaccinations are up to date. On examination he is pale and thin, T 37.6 degC, HR 124, RR 22, BP 100/62, with bilateral cervical and inguinal lymphadenopathy with nodes 1.5-2 cm, non-tender and rubbery. There is hepatomegaly 3 cm below the costal margin and splenomegaly 4 cm. He has scattered petechiae on the lower legs and a few unexplained bruises on the chest. There is no joint swelling or warmth. Bedside FBC requested same-day shows Hb 71 g/L, WCC 24 x10^9/L with 65% blasts on the analyser flag, neutrophils 0.7 x10^9/L, platelets 38 x10^9/L. The GP is arranging onward referral.

data_table.Examination: Pale, generalised non-tender lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly, petechiae and bruising.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 71 g/L, WCC 24 (65% blasts on analyser), neutrophils 0.7, platelets 38.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence empirical oral amoxicillin, repeat FBC at 1 week
options.rationale: Antibiotics and watchful waiting are unsafe; the blast count and pancytopenia require admission and urgent specialist review the same day (RCH CPG: Suspected childhood cancer).

options.text: Outpatient haematology clinic referral, review within 6 weeks
options.rationale: Outpatient timelines miss the critical window for managing tumour lysis and infection in suspected leukaemia; same-day transfer is required (Cancer Council Optimal Care Pathway).
source_refs: Cancer Council Optimal Care Pathway: Childhood ALL

options.text: Same-day phone referral to tertiary paediatric oncology for transfer
options.rationale: Pancytopenia with circulating blasts, lymphadenopathy and hepatosplenomegaly is a clinical and laboratory picture of acute leukaemia; the standard of care is immediate (same-day) telephone referral to the paediatric oncology service for transfer, urgent biochemistry (including tumour lysis labs) and definitive diagnosis with bone marrow aspirate and immunophenotyping (RCH CPG: Suspected childhood cancer; Cancer Council OCP).
source_refs: Cancer Council Optimal Care Pathway: Childhood ALL

options.text: Iron studies, ferritin and reticulocytes; trial oral iron if low
options.rationale: Iron studies do not explain pancytopenia with circulating blasts and lymphadenopathy; iron therapy delays diagnosis of leukaemia (RCH CPG: Iron deficiency).

options.text: Ultrasound of cervical nodes within 2 weeks before any referral
options.rationale: Imaging the nodes does not change immediate management; the blood film and bone marrow obtained at the tertiary centre give the diagnosis (RCH CPG: Suspected childhood cancer).

explanation.summary: Suspected childhood leukaemia warrants same-day phone referral and transfer to a tertiary paediatric oncology service for blood film review, tumour lysis biochemistry and definitive bone marrow assessment. Outpatient or 'wait and see' approaches risk tumour lysis, infection and bleeding (RCH CPG: Suspected childhood cancer; Cancer Council Optimal Care Pathway).
key_points: Pancytopenia + blasts + organomegaly = paediatric oncology phone now
Send tumour lysis panel (urate, K, phosphate, Ca, urea, creat, LDH)
Group and hold, coagulation screen
Avoid IM injections, NSAIDs and aspirin
Pretransfusion samples sent before any blood product is given
explanation.pearls: Night-time bone pain that wakes a young child is leukaemia until proven otherwise.

sources.label: Cancer Council Optimal Care Pathway: Childhood ALL

subtopic_detail: Tumour lysis syndrome - recognition and initial step
stem: A 7-year-old girl with newly diagnosed B-cell ALL is admitted to the paediatric oncology ward. Her presenting WCC was 180 x10^9/L with bulky mediastinal disease and tender hepatosplenomegaly. She has not yet received any chemotherapy. Pre-treatment workup is in progress. She has been on IV hyperhydration with 0.45% saline at 3 L/m^2/24 hours for 6 hours. She is well, alert, with no muscle weakness, no chest pain, no urinary symptoms. Observations: T 37.2 degC, HR 110, RR 22, BP 108/64. Initial biochemistry, returned just now, shows urea 8.6 mmol/L, creatinine 78 micromol/L (above the upper limit for age), potassium 5.8 mmol/L, corrected calcium 2.05 mmol/L, phosphate 2.4 mmol/L, urate 0.62 mmol/L (upper limit 0.36), LDH 1850 U/L. Urine output over the last 6 hours has been 1.2 mL/kg/hour. ECG shows peaked T waves in V2-V4 without QRS widening. No allopurinol has been given. The consultant is on the phone for the next-step discussion before induction is started.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.2 degC, HR 110, RR 22, BP 108/64. ECG: peaked T waves V2-V4.
data_table.Examination: Alert, no muscle weakness, hepatosplenomegaly tender, mediastinal mass on CXR.
data_table.Investigations: Urea 8.6, creat 78, K 5.8, Ca corrected 2.05, phosphate 2.4, urate 0.62 mmol/L, LDH 1850.
data_table.Medications: IV hyperhydration 3 L/m^2/24 h with 0.45% saline. No chemotherapy yet.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate management step?

options.text: Oral allopurinol three times daily plus add potassium to fluids
options.rationale: Allopurinol blocks new urate formation but does not lower existing urate, so it is inadequate when urate is already elevated; adding potassium to fluids in established tumour lysis is dangerous (eviQ Tumour lysis syndrome).

options.text: IV rasburicase, continue hyperhydration, frequent biochemistry, PICU/nephrology input
options.rationale: Laboratory tumour lysis syndrome is established (urate, potassium, phosphate all elevated with corrected calcium low) at high risk (WCC 180, bulky disease, LDH > 2x normal). Australian guidance recommends rasburicase rather than allopurinol when urate is already elevated or risk is high, continued hyperhydration without potassium, frequent monitoring and early PICU/nephrology involvement (eviQ; Coiffier consensus).

options.text: IV calcium gluconate 10% and proceed with induction chemotherapy now
options.rationale: Calcium is given only for symptomatic hypocalcaemia or ECG instability with hyperkalaemia (QRS widening or arrhythmia); routine calcium can precipitate calcium phosphate crystals when phosphate is high. Induction should not start until tumour lysis is controlled (eviQ Tumour lysis syndrome).

options.text: Urgent urine alkalinisation with sodium bicarbonate to pH above 7.5
options.rationale: Urinary alkalinisation is no longer recommended because it promotes calcium phosphate precipitation and xanthine deposition; rasburicase has superseded this approach (eviQ; Coiffier consensus).

options.text: Withhold further IV fluids and observe to avoid cardiac strain
options.rationale: Fluid restriction worsens tumour lysis nephropathy; high-risk TLS is managed with aggressive hyperhydration with strict monitoring of urine output and fluid balance, not restriction (eviQ Tumour lysis syndrome).

explanation.summary: Laboratory tumour lysis syndrome in high-risk leukaemia (high WCC, bulky disease, raised LDH) is managed with hyperhydration without potassium, rasburicase when urate is elevated or risk is high, frequent biochemistry and urine output monitoring, and early PICU and nephrology involvement. Induction is delayed until biochemistry is controlled (eviQ Tumour lysis syndrome).
key_points: TLS labs: urate, K, phosphate up; Ca down; LDH high
High risk: WCC > 100, bulky disease, LDH > 2x ULN
Rasburicase replaces allopurinol when urate already elevated
Hyperhydration without K; avoid bicarbonate
G6PD screen before rasburicase (haemolysis risk)
explanation.pearls: Once urate, K and phosphate move together, you are behind - treat the next hour, not the next day.

subtopic_detail: Toddler iron deficiency - oral iron trial vs investigate
stem: An 18-month-old boy is brought to the GP by his mother, who is concerned about pallor and reduced energy over the past 2 months. He was born at term, breast-fed for the first 6 months and is now on a diet dominated by approximately 800 mL/day of full-cream cow's milk, refused solids, and occasional toast. He has met all developmental milestones but is described as fussier than his older sister. There is no history of jaundice, bone pain, weight loss, night sweats, foreign travel, or contact with TB. There has been no overt blood loss in stool or urine and bowel habit is normal. Family history is unremarkable; both parents are of Anglo-Australian background. He is fully vaccinated. On examination he is alert, plays appropriately, mildly pale, with no scleral icterus, no lymphadenopathy, no hepatosplenomegaly and no bruising. Height and weight are on the 25th centile (stable). Observations: T 36.7 degC, HR 122, RR 26, BP 92/54. Bedside FBC: Hb 86 g/L, MCV 62 fL, MCH 21 pg, RDW 17%, reticulocytes 70 x10^9/L, WCC 8.4 with normal differential, platelets 432 x10^9/L. Iron studies: ferritin 6 micrograms/L, transferrin saturation 5%.

data_table.Examination: Mildly pale, otherwise well, no lymphadenopathy or organomegaly, stable centiles.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 86, MCV 62, MCH 21, RDW 17%, retics 70, platelets 432. Ferritin 6 microg/L, TSat 5%.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Transfuse one paediatric unit of packed red cells as treatment
options.rationale: Asymptomatic chronic iron deficiency anaemia with a haemoglobin of 86 g/L in a haemodynamically stable child does not warrant transfusion; oral iron is appropriate and rapid transfusion in chronic anaemia risks fluid overload (RCH CPG: Iron deficiency).

options.text: Refer urgently for bone marrow aspirate to exclude underlying leukaemia
options.rationale: The clinical picture is benign, with no lymphadenopathy, organomegaly, neutropenia or thrombocytopenia, and the microcytic indices with low ferritin point clearly to iron deficiency; marrow is not indicated (RCH CPG: Suspected childhood cancer).

options.text: Haemoglobin electrophoresis and faecal occult blood testing first-line before iron
options.rationale: Electrophoresis and occult blood are considered if iron deficiency is unexplained, fails an iron trial, or there is high-risk ethnic background or GI symptoms; they are not first-line in a classic dietary toddler (RCH CPG: Iron deficiency).

options.text: Oral elemental iron, reduce cow's milk below 500 mL, recheck FBC at 4 weeks
options.rationale: The biochemical and dietary picture is classical toddler iron deficiency anaemia from cow's milk over-consumption. RCH guidance is a trial of oral elemental iron at 3-6 mg/kg/day plus dietary counselling (limit cow's milk to under 500 mL/day, encourage iron-rich solids) with FBC and ferritin review at 4 weeks (RCH CPG: Iron deficiency).

options.text: Arrange outpatient IV iron polymaltose infusion as first-line treatment now
options.rationale: IV iron is reserved for children who cannot tolerate or do not respond to oral iron, have ongoing loss exceeding intake, or have malabsorption; oral therapy is the first-line treatment (RCH CPG: Iron deficiency; AMH).

explanation.summary: Toddler iron deficiency anaemia driven by excessive cow's milk intake is treated with oral elemental iron 3-6 mg/kg/day, dietary counselling (limit milk under 500 mL/day, iron-rich solids) and a follow-up FBC at 4 weeks. Failure to respond at 4 weeks or atypical features prompts further investigation (RCH CPG: Iron deficiency).
key_points: Oral iron 3-6 mg/kg elemental/day, once daily or alternate days
Cow's milk under 500 mL/day after 12 months
Recheck FBC + reticulocytes at 4 weeks (Hb should rise > 10 g/L)
Investigate further if no response or atypical features
Continue iron 3 months after Hb normalises to refill stores
explanation.pearls: Alternate-day dosing improves absorption and tolerability through reduced hepcidin elevation.

subtopic_detail: When to investigate iron deficiency further
stem: A 4-year-old girl previously diagnosed with iron deficiency anaemia has returned to the GP for a routine review. Three months ago her haemoglobin was 88 g/L with ferritin 7 micrograms/L. She was started on oral elemental iron 4 mg/kg/day with cow's milk reduced to 400 mL/day and was reported by her mother to be taking the iron consistently. Over the last three months she has had two short episodes of loose stools she attributed to gastroenteritis circulating at daycare, and her mother has noted ongoing tiredness and intermittent abdominal bloating. There is no overt blood in the stool, no haematuria, no fevers, no rash and no bone pain. Vaccinations are up to date and growth has dropped from the 25th to the 10th centile for weight over the past year. On examination she looks slim but well, with no lymphadenopathy, no organomegaly and no joint swelling. Observations are within normal limits for age. Repeat FBC today: Hb 92 g/L, MCV 67 fL, RDW 17%, platelets 388, WCC normal. Ferritin 9 micrograms/L. Iron has been continuously prescribed and adherence is reported as good.

data_table.Examination: Slim, no organomegaly, no lymphadenopathy. Weight centile dropped 25th to 10th.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 92 (was 88), MCV 67, ferritin 9 microg/L (was 7), platelets 388. Loose stools intermittently.
data_table.Medications: Oral elemental iron 4 mg/kg/day for 12 weeks.

lead_in: Which investigation is most appropriate now?

options.text: Continue current oral iron and re-test FBC in another 3 months
options.rationale: She has failed an adequate 3-month oral iron trial with dietary counselling, with persistently low ferritin, falling centiles and intermittent loose stools; continuing without investigation is inappropriate (RCH CPG: Iron deficiency).

options.text: Upper GI endoscopy as the first-line investigation
options.rationale: Endoscopy is the diagnostic test if coeliac serology is positive or in the no-biopsy pathway in children meeting specific antibody thresholds; it is not the first investigation (RCH CPG: Coeliac disease).

options.text: Anti-tissue transglutaminase IgA antibodies plus total IgA
options.rationale: Iron deficiency that does not respond to an adequate oral iron trial in the setting of falling growth centiles, intermittent loose stools and bloating is a red flag for coeliac disease; coeliac antibody testing (anti-tTG IgA with total IgA to exclude IgA deficiency) is the recommended first-line investigation while the child remains on a gluten-containing diet (RCH CPG: Coeliac disease; RCH CPG: Iron deficiency).

options.text: Haemoglobin electrophoresis to exclude beta-thalassaemia trait
options.rationale: Electrophoresis is considered for unexplained microcytosis once iron studies are normal; her ferritin remains low, so iron deficiency is the dominant unresolved problem and the underlying cause should be sought (RCH CPG: Iron deficiency).

options.text: Meckel scan with technetium-99m pertechnetate
options.rationale: Meckel scan is indicated for painless overt GI bleeding suspected to be from a Meckel diverticulum; without GI bleeding it is not appropriate first-line in this child (RCH CPG: GI bleeding in children).
source_refs: RCH CPG: GI bleeding in children

explanation.summary: Iron deficiency that fails an adequate oral iron trial of 3 months in a child with falling growth, loose stools or bloating should prompt coeliac screening with anti-tTG IgA and total IgA. Other reasons to investigate include suspected occult bleeding, malabsorption symptoms, or high-risk ethnicity for haemoglobinopathy (RCH CPG: Iron deficiency; RCH CPG: Coeliac disease).
key_points: Investigate iron deficiency if: failed oral trial, faltering growth, GI symptoms, atypical features
First-line for coeliac: anti-tTG IgA + total IgA, on gluten
Confirm coeliac with biopsy (or no-biopsy pathway if criteria met)
Consider H. pylori, occult blood, electrophoresis as situation dictates
Reassess adherence and dose before labelling as treatment-failure
explanation.pearls: Falling centiles plus persistent iron deficiency is coeliac disease until disproven.

sources.label: RCH CPG: GI bleeding in children

stem: A 14-year-old previously well girl presents to her GP with a 6-week history of a painless lump in the left side of her neck that has gradually enlarged. She also describes intermittent night sweats requiring change of clothing and a 3 kg unintentional weight loss over the same period. There has been no preceding sore throat, no dental infection and no foreign travel. She has had no fevers documented at home, no cough, no dyspnoea, no abdominal pain or pruritus. She has had no contact with tuberculosis or unwell pets and consumes no unpasteurised dairy. She is HPV-vaccinated and otherwise immunised. On examination she is well, afebrile, with a 3 cm firm, rubbery, non-tender, mobile node in the left posterior triangle, with a second 1.5 cm node deeper in the supraclavicular fossa on the same side. There is no other regional or generalised lymphadenopathy, no hepatosplenomegaly and no skin changes. Bedside FBC shows Hb 118 g/L, WCC 7.4 with normal differential, platelets 280. CRP is 14, LDH 320 U/L (upper limit 250). ESR is 56. A bedside ENT examination of the oropharynx is normal.

data_table.Examination: 3 cm rubbery non-tender mobile left posterior triangle node + 1.5 cm left supraclavicular node, no organomegaly.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 118, WCC 7.4 (normal diff), platelets 280, CRP 14, LDH 320, ESR 56.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Trial 2 weeks of oral cefalexin and clinical review
options.rationale: Empirical antibiotics are appropriate for tender, fluctuant or rapidly enlarging nodes with clinical signs of bacterial lymphadenitis; this presentation has none of those features and a trial would delay diagnosis (RCH CPG: Lymphadenopathy).

options.text: Ultrasound of the neck nodes only, review in 6 weeks
options.rationale: Ultrasound may help characterise nodes but does not diagnose lymphoma, and 6-week observation in the face of red flags (size, supraclavicular site, B symptoms, raised LDH) is unsafe (RCH CPG: Lymphadenopathy).

options.text: Fine-needle aspiration cytology of the largest neck node
options.rationale: FNA cannot reliably diagnose or subtype lymphoma because architecture is required; excisional biopsy is the standard (eviQ; Cancer Council Optimal Care Pathway).
source_refs: Cancer Council Optimal Care Pathway: Hodgkin lymphoma

options.text: Bartonella, EBV, toxoplasma serology with review in 4 weeks
options.rationale: Infective serology is reasonable for atypical reactive lymphadenopathy but does not justify deferring excisional biopsy when red flags are present; pursue both in parallel rather than delaying referral (RCH CPG: Lymphadenopathy).

options.text: Urgent paediatric oncology referral for excisional node biopsy and staging
options.rationale: She has multiple red flags for lymphoma: supraclavicular location, node larger than 2 cm, persistent for more than 4-6 weeks, non-tender, plus B symptoms (night sweats, unintentional weight loss) and elevated LDH and ESR. Australian and international guidance recommend urgent referral for excisional biopsy rather than FNA, plus staging (CT/PET) coordinated by oncology (RCH CPG: Lymphadenopathy; eviQ; Cancer Council OCP).
source_refs: Cancer Council Optimal Care Pathway: Hodgkin lymphoma

explanation.summary: Red flags for malignant lymphadenopathy in children and adolescents are: node > 2 cm, supraclavicular location, persistence > 4-6 weeks, rubbery non-tender consistency, B symptoms (fever, night sweats, weight loss > 10%), hepatosplenomegaly, mediastinal mass, elevated LDH or ESR. Any combination warrants urgent referral for excisional biopsy (RCH CPG: Lymphadenopathy).
key_points: Supraclavicular node = biopsy regardless of other features
Size > 2 cm + > 4-6 weeks duration = biopsy
B symptoms: fever > 38, night sweats, unintentional weight loss > 10%
Excisional biopsy, not FNA, for suspected lymphoma
Send LDH, ESR, CXR for mediastinal mass while awaiting biopsy
explanation.pearls: An adolescent supraclavicular node is malignant until proven otherwise - do not start antibiotics and wait.

sources.label: Cancer Council Optimal Care Pathway: Hodgkin lymphoma

subtopic_detail: SCID red flags - recognition and urgent investigation
stem: A 4-month-old boy is brought to a regional ED for his third admission, this time with 10 days of persistent oral thrush despite two courses of oral nystatin, an erythematous nappy-area candidiasis, and a 5-week history of chronic non-bloody diarrhoea with poor weight gain (current weight on the 3rd centile, having tracked along the 50th at birth). His past admissions in the last 6 weeks have been for pneumocystis-like interstitial pneumonia requiring nasal high-flow and one episode of CMV viraemia. He was born at term to non-consanguineous parents; his mother had a brother who died of an unexplained pneumonia at 7 months of age. He received BCG at birth in his country of birth, and rotavirus oral vaccine at 6 weeks in Australia. On examination he is small and pale, with oral plaques, perineal candidiasis, no thymic shadow on a recent chest X-ray, no palpable lymph nodes or tonsillar tissue, and bilateral fine inspiratory crackles. T 37.4 degC, HR 152, RR 50, SpO2 94% RA. FBC today: Hb 96, WCC 5.2 with absolute lymphocyte count 0.6 x10^9/L (well below the age-specific lower limit of around 3.0). Total IgG, IgA, IgM are all low. The paediatric team is concerned about a primary immunodeficiency.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.4 degC, HR 152, RR 50, SpO2 94% RA.
data_table.Examination: Failure to thrive, oral thrush, perineal candidiasis, no thymic shadow on CXR, absent tonsils/lymph nodes, bilateral crackles.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 96, WCC 5.2, ALC 0.6 x10^9/L, IgG / IgA / IgM all low.
data_table.Medications: Recent rotavirus oral vaccine, BCG at birth overseas.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate single next step?

options.text: Empirical oral fluconazole and clinic review in 4 weeks
options.rationale: Persistent candidiasis is the surface feature of a likely SCID; oral fluconazole alone misses the underlying immunodeficiency and the time-critical need for transplant pathway evaluation (ASCIA: SCID).

options.text: Same-day immunology referral, lymphocyte subsets, isolation, no live vaccines
options.rationale: The presentation is highly suspicious for severe combined immunodeficiency: failure to thrive, severe lymphopenia (ALC < 2.5-3.0), opportunistic infection (Pneumocystis-like pneumonia, CMV viraemia, persistent candidiasis), maternal family history of unexplained infant death, BCG exposure, and absent thymic shadow. Urgent immunology referral, T/B/NK lymphocyte subsets, reverse isolation, withholding live vaccines and use of irradiated CMV-negative leucodepleted blood products are time-critical (ASCIA: SCID; eviQ).

options.text: Reassure parents that recurrent infections are normal at 4 months
options.rationale: Pneumocystis-like pneumonia, CMV viraemia, FTT, persistent candidiasis and severe lymphopenia together are not normal at any age and warrant urgent specialist evaluation (RCH CPG: Recurrent infections; ASCIA).

options.text: Give routine 6-month MMR catch-up vaccination at this visit
options.rationale: Live vaccines including MMR, rotavirus, BCG and varicella are contraindicated in suspected SCID and cause disseminated infection; the immediate priority is to stop further live vaccine exposure and confirm the diagnosis (ASCIA: SCID; NCIRS).

options.text: Send HIV antibody (not PCR) and clinic review in 2 weeks
options.rationale: Maternal-acquired HIV antibody persists in infants under 18 months irrespective of HIV status; HIV PCR (proviral DNA or RNA) is the test of choice and should be sent in parallel to immunology workup, not as a stand-alone next step (RCH CPG: HIV in children).

explanation.summary: SCID is a paediatric emergency. Red flags include severe lymphopenia (ALC under age-specific lower limit, often below 2.5 x10^9/L), failure to thrive, opportunistic infection (PCP, CMV, persistent candidiasis), absent thymic shadow, and family history of early unexplained infant death. Urgent immunology referral, reverse isolation, withholding live vaccines, and irradiated CMV-negative leucodepleted blood products are time-critical; haematopoietic stem cell transplant in the first 3.5 months of life carries a markedly better prognosis (ASCIA: SCID).
key_points: Severe lymphopenia at any age: think SCID and check subsets
Withhold live vaccines: rotavirus, BCG, MMR, varicella
Use irradiated CMV-negative leucodepleted blood products
Early HSCT before 3.5 months improves survival markedly
Family history of unexplained infant infectious death is a red flag
explanation.pearls: If a 4-month-old has had Pneumocystis pneumonia, the diagnosis is SCID until immunology proves otherwise.
